examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize empty set. Step 2: Call backtrack(0). Start=0, i=0: swap nums[0]=1 with nums[0]=1 → [1,1,2], backtrack(1). Start=1, i=1: swap nums[1]=1 with nums[1]=1 → [1,1,2], backtrack(2). Start=2: add tuple([1,1,2]) to set. Backtrack: swap back. Start=1, i=2: swap nums[1]=1 with nums[2]=2 → [1,2,1], backtrack(2). Start=2: add tuple([1,2,1]) to set. Backtrack: swap back. Start=0, i=1: swap nums[0]=1 with nums[1]=1 → [1,1,2] (duplicate path), backtrack(1)... generates same permutations. Start=0, i=2: swap nums[0]=1 with nums[2]=2 → [2,1,1], backtrack(1). Start=1, i=1: swap nums[1]=1 with nums[1]=1 → [2,1,1], backtrack(2): add tuple([2,1,1]) to set. Start=1, i=2: swap nums[1]=1 with nums[2]=1 → [2,1,1] (duplicate), backtrack(2): adds duplicate. Final set: {(1,1,2),(1,2,1),(2,1,1)}. Convert to list of lists.

Step 1: Initialize empty set. Step 2: backtrack(0). Start=0: i=0 → [1,2,3], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [1,2,3], backtrack(2): add (1,2,3). i=2 → [1,3,2], backtrack(2): add (1,3,2). Start=0: i=1 → [2,1,3], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [2,1,3], backtrack(2): add (2,1,3). i=2 → [2,3,1], backtrack(2): add (2,3,1). Start=0: i=2 → [3,2,1], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [3,2,1], backtrack(2): add (3,2,1). i=2 → [3,1,2], backtrack(2): add (3,1,2). Final set has 6 tuples. Convert to lists.

approaches.name: Brute Force with Set O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations using backtracking without pruning duplicates, then filter unique ones using a set. This is straightforward but inefficient due to redundant work.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique Permutations with Set Filtering
ladders.desc: Complete solution generating all permutations via backtracking and filtering duplicates with a set.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Permutations II problem by first generating all possible permutations of the input array using backtracking, then filtering out duplicates using a set data structure. The intuition is straightforward: generate every possible arrangement of elements, then remove duplicates by storing results in a set (which automatically handles uniqueness). While conceptually simple, this method is inefficient because it generates n! permutations even when there are duplicates, leading to redundant work. The backtracking algorithm works by recursively swapping elements: at each position, we try placing every element from the current position to the end, then recursively generate permutations for the remaining positions. After generating a complete permutation, we convert it to a tuple (since lists aren't hashable) and add it to a set. Finally, we convert the set back to a list of lists. Time complexity is O(n! * n) because we generate n! permutations and each permutation takes O(n) to copy/store. Space complexity is O(n! * n) for storing all permutations. This connects to L1 by using the permutation generation subroutine as the core component. Common mistakes include forgetting to convert lists to tuples for set storage, not handling empty inputs properly, and inefficiently copying arrays. Real code pattern: def permuteUnique(nums): result_set = set(); def backtrack(start): if start == len(nums): result_set.add(tuple(nums[:])); return; for i in range(start, len(nums)): nums[start], nums[i] = nums[i], nums[start]; backtrack(start + 1); nums[start], nums[i] = nums[i], nums[start]; backtrack(0); return [list(perm) for perm in result_set]

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty set. Step 2: Call backtrack(0). Start=0, i=0: swap nums[0]=1 with nums[0]=1 → [1,1,2], backtrack(1). Start=1, i=1: swap nums[1]=1 with nums[1]=1 → [1,1,2], backtrack(2). Start=2: add tuple([1,1,2]) to set. Backtrack: swap back. Start=1, i=2: swap nums[1]=1 with nums[2]=2 → [1,2,1], backtrack(2). Start=2: add tuple([1,2,1]) to set. Backtrack: swap back. Start=0, i=1: swap nums[0]=1 with nums[1]=1 → [1,1,2] (duplicate path), backtrack(1)... generates same permutations. Start=0, i=2: swap nums[0]=1 with nums[2]=2 → [2,1,1], backtrack(1). Start=1, i=1: swap nums[1]=1 with nums[1]=1 → [2,1,1], backtrack(2): add tuple([2,1,1]) to set. Start=1, i=2: swap nums[1]=1 with nums[2]=1 → [2,1,1] (duplicate), backtrack(2): adds duplicate. Final set: {(1,1,2),(1,2,1),(2,1,1)}. Convert to list of lists.

Step 1: Initialize empty set. Step 2: backtrack(0). Start=0: i=0 → [1,2,3], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [1,2,3], backtrack(2): add (1,2,3). i=2 → [1,3,2], backtrack(2): add (1,3,2). Start=0: i=1 → [2,1,3], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [2,1,3], backtrack(2): add (2,1,3). i=2 → [2,3,1], backtrack(2): add (2,3,1). Start=0: i=2 → [3,2,1], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [3,2,1], backtrack(2): add (3,2,1). i=2 → [3,1,2], backtrack(2): add (3,1,2). Final set has 6 tuples. Convert to lists.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Permutations via Backtracking
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of an array using recursive backtracking with swapping.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine generates all permutations of an input array using a backtracking algorithm that swaps elements. The intuition is to build permutations by fixing elements at each position: starting from the first position, we try every possible element by swapping it into that position, then recursively generate permutations for the remaining positions. This is a classic depth-first search approach where each recursive call advances one position deeper into the array. The algorithm works by maintaining a current index 'start': when start reaches the array length, we've completed a permutation and add a copy to our results. Otherwise, we iterate i from start to len(nums)-1, swap nums[start] with nums[i], recursively call backtrack(start+1), then swap back to restore the array for the next iteration (this is the backtracking step). Time complexity is O(n! * n) because we generate n! permutations and each takes O(n) time for copying. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack (depth n). This connects to L0 by being the core permutation generator that gets wrapped with duplicate filtering. Common mistakes include not making copies of completed permutations (leading to all results being the same reference), forgetting the swap-back step, and incorrect base case handling. Real code pattern: def generate_permutations(nums): result = []; def backtrack(start): if start == len(nums): result.append(nums[:]); return; for i in range(start, len(nums)): nums[start], nums[i] = nums[i], nums[start]; backtrack(start + 1); nums[start], nums[i] = nums[i], nums[start]; backtrack(0); return result

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty result list. Step 2: backtrack(0). Start=0, i=0: swap nums[0]=1 with nums[0]=1 → [1,2], backtrack(1). Start=1: base case reached, add copy [1,2] to result. Return, swap back. Start=0, i=1: swap nums[0]=1 with nums[1]=2 → [2,1], backtrack(1). Start=1: add copy [2,1] to result. Return, swap back. Final result: [[1,2],[2,1]].

Step 1: Initialize result. Step 2: backtrack(0). Start=0: i=0 → [1,2,3], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [1,2,3], backtrack(2): add [1,2,3]. i=2 → [1,3,2], backtrack(2): add [1,3,2]. i=1 → [2,1,3], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [2,1,3], backtrack(2): add [2,1,3]. i=2 → [2,3,1], backtrack(2): add [2,3,1]. i=2 → [3,2,1], backtrack(1). Start=1: i=1 → [3,2,1], backtrack(2): add [3,2,1]. i=2 → [3,1,2], backtrack(2): add [3,1,2]. Final result has 6 permutations.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking with Element Swapping
ladders.desc: The recursive swapping technique that explores all arrangements by fixing positions.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the backtracking mechanism using element swapping to generate permutations. The intuition is based on systematically exploring the solution space: we treat permutation generation as placing elements in positions. At each recursive call, we're responsible for filling positions from 'start' to the end. We fix the element at position 'start' by swapping it with each possible candidate from positions start to end, then recursively handle the remaining positions. The key insight is that by swapping, we efficiently reuse the same array without needing extra space for tracking used elements. The algorithm: if start equals array length, we've completed a permutation. Otherwise, for i from start to len(nums)-1: swap nums[start] and nums[i], recursively call with start+1, then swap back to restore the state for the next candidate. This creates a depth-first search tree where each path from root to leaf represents one permutation. Time complexity for generating all permutations is O(n! * n) due to n! leaves and O(n) work per leaf. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L1 as the complete implementation of the permutation generator. Common mistakes include modifying the array in place without proper restoration, incorrect loop ranges, and misunderstanding the base case. Real code pattern: def backtrack(start): if start == len(nums): # process complete permutation; for i in range(start, len(nums)): swap(nums[start], nums[i]); backtrack(start + 1); swap(nums[start], nums[i]) # backtrack

examples.trace: Initial: [A,B]. Call backtrack(0). start=0 ≠ 2. Loop i=0: swap A with A → [A,B]. Recursive backtrack(1). start=1 ≠ 2. Loop i=1: swap B with B → [A,B]. Recursive backtrack(2): start=2 == 2, process permutation [A,B]. Return, swap back B with B. Return, swap back A with A. Loop i=1: swap A with B → [B,A]. Recursive backtrack(1). start=1 ≠ 2. Loop i=1: swap A with A → [B,A]. Recursive backtrack(2): process [B,A]. Return, swap back A with A. Return, swap back A with B to restore [A,B]. Done.

examples.output: First few levels of recursion tree
examples.trace: backtrack(0): i=0 → swap 1 with 1 → [1,2,3], backtrack(1). backtrack(1): i=1 → swap 2 with 2 → [1,2,3], backtrack(2). backtrack(2): i=2 → swap 3 with 3 → [1,2,3], backtrack(3): process [1,2,3]. Backtrack: swap back, return. backtrack(1): i=2 → swap 2 with 3 → [1,3,2], backtrack(2). backtrack(2): i=2 → swap 2 with 2 → [1,3,2], backtrack(3): process [1,3,2]. Continue for other branches.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Swapping and Recursive State Management
ladders.desc: How to swap array elements and manage state in recursion for combinatorial generation.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers two fundamental techniques: swapping elements in an array and managing state during recursion. Swapping is used to place different elements at each position during permutation generation. The operation nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i] exchanges elements at indices i and j in constant time. Recursive state management involves preserving and restoring the array state: before each recursive call, we modify the array by swapping; after the recursive call returns, we swap back to restore the original arrangement so the next iteration can proceed correctly. This is crucial for backtracking algorithms. The intuition is that recursion explores one branch of possibilities, and we need to clean up changes before exploring sibling branches. The algorithm for a single swap-and-recurse step: swap elements at positions start and i; make recursive call to explore deeper; swap back to original positions. Time complexity per swap is O(1), and space complexity is O(1) aside from recursion overhead. This connects to L2 as the mechanism enabling the backtracking logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to swap back (which corrupts the array), swapping incorrect indices, and not copying the array when needed for results. Real code pattern: # Swap nums[start], nums[i] = nums[i], nums[start]; # Recurse backtrack(start + 1); # Swap back nums[start], nums[i] = nums[i], nums[start]

examples.input: Swap indices 0 and 1 in [A,B,C], then recurse, then swap back
examples.trace: Initial array: [A,B,C]. Swap nums[0] and nums[1]: becomes [B,A,C]. Make recursive call (explore subtree). After recursive call returns, array is still [B,A,C]. Swap back nums[0] and nums[1]: becomes [A,B,C] (original state). Now ready for next iteration.

examples.input: Full cycle for start=0, i=0,1,2 with [1,2,3]
examples.trace: Start: [1,2,3]. i=0: swap 0 with 0 → [1,2,3], recurse, after return swap back → [1,2,3]. i=1: swap 0 with 1 → [2,1,3], recurse, after return swap back → [1,2,3]. i=2: swap 0 with 2 → [3,2,1], recurse, after return swap back → [1,2,3]. Array ends as original.

testCases.input: Swap indices 0 and 1 then swap back in [A,B]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Swapping
ladders.desc: How to exchange two elements in an array using tuple assignment.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers swapping two elements in an array, which is fundamental to the permutation algorithm. Swapping exchanges the values at two specified indices in the array. In Python-style code, this is done using tuple assignment: nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]. This works by evaluating the right-hand side first (creating a tuple of the values), then assigning them to the left-hand side indices. The intuition is that we need to temporarily place an element at a different position to explore a different arrangement. The algorithm: store the value at index i in a temporary variable, assign value at j to index i, then assign the temporary value to index j. However, Python's tuple assignment handles this automatically. Time complexity is O(1) since it's a constant-time operation. Space complexity is O(1) for the temporary values. This connects to L3 as the core operation used in state management. Common mistakes include using incorrect indices, trying to swap with out-of-bounds indices, or misunderstanding that tuple assignment evaluates the right side first. Real code pattern: # Swap elements at indices i and j: nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]

examples.trace: Step 1: Evaluate right side: (nums[1], nums[0]) = (20, 10). Step 2: Assign to left side: nums[0] = 20, nums[1] = 10. Array is now [20, 10, 30].
Step 1: Right side: (nums[2], nums[1]) = (5, 5). Step 2: Assign: nums[1] = 5, nums[2] = 5. No change as all elements are equal.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Permutation?
ladders.desc: Understanding permutations as all possible arrangements of elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept explains what a permutation is: a permutation of a collection of elements is any possible arrangement or ordering of those elements. For example, given elements [A, B, C], the permutations are [A,B,C], [A,C,B], [B,A,C], [B,C,A], [C,A,B], [C,B,A]. The key intuition is that order matters: [A,B] is different from [B,A]. When elements can repeat (like [A,A,B]), permutations with duplicates are considered the same if they look identical, so [A,A,B] and [A,A,B] (with the same order) are not distinct. The number of distinct permutations for n unique elements is n! (n factorial). If there are duplicates, the count is reduced. This concept is fundamental because the problem asks to generate all unique permutations. Understanding this sets the stage for why we need algorithms to generate these arrangements and why filtering duplicates is necessary. This connects to L4 as the goal of the swapping operations. Common mistakes include confusing permutations with combinations (where order doesn't matter), miscounting permutations with duplicates, and not considering empty arrays. Real code pattern: No code here—this is a conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Step 1: List all possible orders. Option 1: keep order [1,2]. Option 2: swap to get [2,1]. That's all possibilities.
Step 1: Consider all arrangements: [1,1,2], [1,2,1], [2,1,1]. Note that [1,1,2] and [1,1,2] (from swapping identical 1's) are the same, so we have only 3 unique ones.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Sorting and Skipping O(n * n!)
approaches.summary: Sort the input array first, then use backtracking while skipping duplicate elements at each step to ensure unique permutations. This avoids generating duplicates by pruning the search space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique Permutations with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution to generate all unique permutations of an array with duplicates using backtracking with sorting and skipping.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the Permutations II problem. The intuition is to generate permutations systematically while avoiding duplicates by pruning the search space. First, we sort the input array so that duplicates become adjacent, making them easy to identify. Then, we use backtracking to build permutations recursively. At each step, we iterate through available elements, but when we encounter a duplicate element (i.e., nums[i] == nums[i-1]), we skip it if the previous duplicate hasn't been used in the current recursion path (tracked via a used array). This ensures we only generate each unique permutation once. The algorithm works by maintaining a current permutation (path) and a boolean array (used) to track which elements are already included. We recursively add elements to the path, and when the path length equals the input length, we add it to the result. The sorting step is crucial because it groups duplicates together, allowing us to skip them efficiently during backtracking. Time complexity is O(n * n!) because there are n! permutations in the worst case (when all elements are unique), and each permutation takes O(n) to build. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and auxiliary arrays. Connection to L1: This level implements the full backtracking function from L1 within the main solution framework. Common mistakes include forgetting to sort the array, incorrectly handling the skipping condition (e.g., skipping all duplicates instead of only when the previous duplicate is unused), or not resetting the used array properly. Real code pattern: def permuteUnique(nums): nums.sort(); result = []; used = [False]*len(nums); def backtrack(path): if len(path) == len(nums): result.append(path[:]); return; for i in range(len(nums)): if used[i] or (i>0 and nums[i]==nums[i-1] and not used[i-1]): continue; used[i] = True; path.append(nums[i]); backtrack(path); path.pop(); used[i] = False; backtrack([]); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort input -> [1,1,2]. Initialize result=[], used=[F,F,F], path=[]. Step 2: Start backtrack. i=0: nums[0]=1, used[0]=F, no skip condition (i=0), so use it. used[0]=T, path=[1]. Recursively backtrack. Step 3: In recursion, i=0: nums[0]=1, used[0]=T, skip. i=1: nums[1]=1, used[1]=F, check skip: i>0 and nums[1]==nums[0] and not used[0]? used[0]=T, so condition false, use it. used[1]=T, path=[1,1]. Recursively backtrack. Step 4: In next recursion, i=0: used[0]=T, skip. i=1: used[1]=T, skip. i=2: nums[2]=2, used[2]=F, no skip, use it. used[2]=T, path=[1,1,2]. Length=3, add to result: [[1,1,2]]. Backtrack: pop 2, used[2]=F. Backtrack: pop second 1, used[1]=F. Step 5: Back to i=2 in first recursion: nums[2]=2, used[2]=F, no skip, use it. used[2]=T, path=[1,2]. Recursively backtrack. Step 6: In recursion, i=0: nums[0]=1, used[0]=T, skip. i=1: nums[1]=1, used[1]=F, check skip: i>0 and nums[1]==nums[0] and not used[0]? used[0]=T, so false, use it. used[1]=T, path=[1,2,1]. Length=3, add to result: [[1,1,2],[1,2,1]]. Backtrack: pop 1, used[1]=F. Step 7: i=2: used[2]=T, skip. Backtrack: pop 2, used[2]=F. Step 8: Back to initial, i=1: nums[1]=1, used[1]=F, check skip: i>0 and nums[1]==nums[0] and not used[0]? used[0]=F (since we backtracked), so true, skip this duplicate. i=2: nums[2]=2, used[2]=F, no skip, use it. used[2]=T, path=[2]. Recursively backtrack. Step 9: In recursion, i=0: nums[0]=1, used[0]=F, no skip, use it. used[0]=T, path=[2,1]. Recursively backtrack. Step 10: In next recursion, i=0: used[0]=T, skip. i=1: nums[1]=1, used[1]=F, check skip: i>0 and nums[1]==nums[0] and not used[0]? used[0]=T, so false, use it. used[1]=T, path=[2,1,1]. Length=3, add to result: [[1,1,2],[1,2,1],[2,1,1]]. Done.

Step 1: Sort input -> [1,2,3] (already sorted). Initialize result=[], used=[F,F,F], path=[]. Step 2: Start backtrack. i=0: nums[0]=1, use it. used[0]=T, path=[1]. Recursively backtrack. Step 3: In recursion, i=0: used[0]=T, skip. i=1: nums[1]=2, use it. used[1]=T, path=[1,2]. Recursively backtrack. Step 4: In next recursion, i=0: used[0]=T, skip. i=1: used[1]=T, skip. i=2: nums[2]=3, use it. path=[1,2,3], add to result: [[1,2,3]]. Backtrack. Step 5: Back to i=2 in previous recursion: nums[2]=3, use it. used[2]=T, path=[1,3]. Recursively backtrack. Step 6: In recursion, i=0: used[0]=T, skip. i=1: nums[1]=2, use it. path=[1,3,2], add: [[1,2,3],[1,3,2]]. Backtrack. Step 7: Continue similarly for starting with 2 and 3. Full trace yields all 6 permutations without duplicates (since no duplicates in input). Final result as above.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Function with Duplicate Skipping
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive backtracking function that builds permutations while skipping duplicates using a used array.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core backtracking function that generates permutations. The intuition is to explore all possible arrangements recursively, but skip duplicate elements to avoid generating the same permutation multiple times. We use a used boolean array to track which elements are already included in the current path. At each recursive call, we iterate through all elements. For each element, we check two conditions: if it's already used (used[i] is true), we skip it. If it's a duplicate (i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1]), we skip it only if the previous duplicate (nums[i-1]) hasn't been used in this recursion path (i.e., not used[i-1]). This condition ensures that we include duplicates in a fixed order: we only use a duplicate if the previous identical element has already been used, preventing different sequences that would lead to the same permutation. The algorithm: function backtrack(path): if path length equals nums length: add copy of path to result; return. for i from 0 to len(nums)-1: if used[i] or (i>0 and nums[i]==nums[i-1] and not used[i-1]): continue. used[i] = true; path.append(nums[i]); backtrack(path); path.pop(); used[i] = false. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n! * n) in the worst case, as it generates all permutations. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and auxiliary arrays. Connection to L0: This function is called within the main solution after sorting the array. Connection to L2: This implements the skipping logic from L2. Common mistakes: Incorrect skip condition (e.g., skipping when used[i-1] is true instead of false), not copying the path when adding to result (leading to references), or forgetting to reset used array after recursion. Real code pattern: def backtrack(path, used, nums, result): if len(path) == len(nums): result.append(path[:]); return; for i in range(len(nums)): if used[i] or (i>0 and nums[i]==nums[i-1] and not used[i-1]): continue; used[i] = True; path.append(nums[i]); backtrack(path, used, nums, result); path.pop(); used[i] = False.

examples.trace: Start: path=[], used=[F,F,F]. Call backtrack. Loop i=0: nums[0]=1, used[0]=F, skip condition? i=0 so no duplicate check. Use it: used[0]=T, path=[1]. Recursive call. In recursion: path=[1], used=[T,F,F]. Loop i=0: used[0]=T, skip. i=1: nums[1]=1, used[1]=F, skip condition? i>0 and nums[1]==nums[0] and not used[0]? used[0]=T, so false. Use it: used[1]=T, path=[1,1]. Recursive call. In next recursion: path=[1,1], used=[T,T,F]. Loop i=0: used[0]=T, skip. i=1: used[1]=T, skip. i=2: nums[2]=2, used[2]=F, no skip. Use it: path=[1,1,2], length=3, add to result. Backtrack: pop 2, used[2]=F. Backtrack: pop second 1, used[1]=F. Back to first recursion: i=2: nums[2]=2, used[2]=F, use it: path=[1,2]. Recursive call... continues similarly to generate all paths.

Start: path=[], used=[F,F,F]. Call backtrack. Loop i=0: use 1, path=[1]. Recursive: i=0 skip, i=1 use 2, path=[1,2]. Recursive: i=0 skip, i=1 skip, i=2 use 3, path=[1,2,3], add. Backtrack, try i=2 in previous: use 3, path=[1,3]. Recursive: i=0 skip, i=1 use 2, path=[1,3,2], add. Continue for starting with 2 and 3. No skips since no duplicates.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Skipping Duplicates in Sorted Array During Backtracking
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the condition to skip duplicate elements to ensure unique permutations.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the key insight: how to skip duplicates during backtracking to avoid generating duplicate permutations. The intuition is that when the array is sorted, identical elements are adjacent. To generate unique permutations, we need to ensure that duplicate elements are used in a consistent order. Specifically, when we have consecutive duplicate elements nums[i] and nums[i-1], we should only include nums[i] in the current path if nums[i-1] has already been used. This prevents us from generating permutations where the order of identical elements is swapped, which would result in the same permutation. The condition is: if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1] and not used[i-1], then skip nums[i]. Here, used[i-1] indicates whether the previous duplicate element is already part of the current path. If it's not used, skipping ensures we don't start a new branch with this duplicate before the previous one is used, maintaining a fixed sequence for duplicates. This pruning reduces the search space significantly when duplicates are present. Step-by-step: 1. Sort the array (prerequisite). 2. During backtracking, for each index i, check if the element is a duplicate of the previous one. 3. If it is a duplicate and the previous duplicate is not yet used, skip this element. 4. Otherwise, proceed to use it. Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per element check, but it integrates into the overall O(n * n!) algorithm. Space complexity is O(1) extra per check. Connection to L1: This condition is used within the backtracking loop. Connection to L3: Relies on sorting from L3 to make duplicates adjacent. Common mistakes: Using the wrong boolean condition (e.g., skipping when used[i-1] is true), not handling the base case i=0, or applying the condition without sorting first. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(nums)): if used[i]: continue; if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1] and not used[i-1]: continue; # proceed to use element.

examples.input: Sorted nums=[1,1,2], at i=1, used=[T,F,F] (first 1 used)
examples.trace: Check condition for i=1: i>0? yes. nums[1]==nums[0]? yes (both 1). not used[0]? used[0] is True, so not used[0] is False. Condition is false, so we do not skip. This allows using the second 1 after the first one is used, generating [1,1,2].

examples.input: Sorted nums=[1,1,2], at i=1, used=[F,F,F] (no elements used yet)
examples.trace: Check condition for i=1: i>0? yes. nums[1]==nums[0]? yes. not used[0]? used[0] is False, so not used[0] is True. Condition is true, so we skip nums[1]. This prevents starting a path with the second 1 before the first, avoiding duplicate permutations like starting with the second 1 then first 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting the Input Array
ladders.desc: Sort the array to group duplicate elements together, enabling efficient duplicate skipping.
ladders.explanation: This level involves sorting the input array as a prerequisite for the duplicate skipping logic. The intuition is that by sorting, we bring all identical elements next to each other, making it easy to identify and handle duplicates during backtracking. Without sorting, duplicates might be scattered, and we would need more complex checks (like using a set) to avoid generating duplicate permutations, which could be less efficient. Sorting transforms the problem into one where we can use a simple adjacent comparison to skip duplicates. The algorithm is straightforward: use any efficient sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort, mergesort) to sort the array in non-decreasing order. For example, if input is [2,1,1], sorting gives [1,1,2]. This ensures that when we iterate through the array during backtracking, we encounter duplicates consecutively, allowing the skip condition (nums[i] == nums[i-1]) to work correctly. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting, which is dominated by the O(n * n!) backtracking, so it doesn't affect the overall asymptotic complexity. Space complexity is O(1) for in-place sorting or O(n) if using extra space. Connection to L2: Sorting enables the duplicate skipping logic by grouping duplicates. Connection to L4: Uses comparison operations from L4. Common mistakes: Forgetting to sort, sorting in descending order (which still groups duplicates but might affect order of permutations), or modifying the original array if it shouldn't be changed (can be handled by copying). Real code pattern: nums.sort() # in-place sort in Python, or sorted_nums = sorted(nums) for a copy.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array: [2,1,1]. Step 2: Apply sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort). Compare elements: pivot 2, partition: less than 2: [1,1], greater: []. Recursively sort [1,1]: already sorted. Combine: [1,1,2]. Step 3: Sorted array: [1,1,2]. Now duplicates 1 are adjacent.

Step 1: Input: [3,1,2,3,1]. Step 2: Sort using mergesort: split into [3,1,2] and [3,1]. Sort left: [1,2,3]. Sort right: [1,3]. Merge: compare 1 and 1, take 1, then 1, then 2 and 3, take 2, then 3 and 3, take both. Result: [1,1,2,3,3]. Step 3: Duplicates 1 and 3 are now grouped.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements and Managing a Used Boolean Array
ladders.desc: Compare elements for equality and maintain a boolean array to track used elements in backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This level covers two fundamental operations: comparing array elements for equality and managing a boolean array to track which elements are used during recursion. The intuition is that these operations are essential for implementing the duplicate skipping and backtracking logic. First, comparing elements: we need to check if two elements are equal (e.g., nums[i] == nums[i-1]) to identify duplicates. This is a simple value comparison that works for integers or other comparable types. Second, the used boolean array: we maintain an array of the same length as the input, where used[i] is true if the element at index i is already included in the current permutation path, and false otherwise. This helps avoid reusing the same element multiple times in a single permutation. During backtracking, before adding an element, we check if used[i] is false; if true, we skip it. After using an element, we set used[i] to true, recurse, and then set it back to false (backtrack) to allow it to be used in other branches. These operations are O(1) time each. The used array requires O(n) space. Connection to L3: Comparing elements is used in sorting and duplicate checks; the used array is integral to backtracking. Connection to L5: Builds on array indexing and boolean logic. Common mistakes: Not initializing the used array properly (all false), forgetting to reset used[i] after recursion (leading to incorrect state), or using the wrong index in comparisons. Real code pattern: used = [False] * len(nums); if nums[i] == nums[i-1]: # comparison; used[i] = True; # mark used; used[i] = False; # unmark.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access nums[0] = 1, nums[1] = 1. Step 2: Compare using == operator: 1 == 1 evaluates to True. Step 3: Result indicates duplicate elements.

examples.input: used array of length 3, mark index 1 as used, then unmark
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize used = [False, False, False]. Step 2: Mark index 1 as used: set used[1] = True. Now used = [False, True, False]. Step 3: After recursion, unmark: set used[1] = False. Used returns to [False, False, False].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Boolean Values
ladders.desc: Understand how to access array elements by index and work with boolean true/false values.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational level for beginners, covering basic array indexing and boolean operations. The intuition is that arrays store elements in ordered positions, and we can access each element using its index (starting from 0). Boolean values represent true or false, often used for conditions and flags. For arrays: given an array nums, nums[i] retrieves the element at position i. For example, if nums = [10, 20, 30], then nums[0] is 10, nums[1] is 20, etc. Indices must be within the range 0 to len(nums)-1 to avoid errors. For booleans: they are logical values, typically represented as True and False in code. We use them in conditions (e.g., if condition: ...) and to store states (e.g., used flags). Operations include assignment (used = True), comparison (==, !=), and logical operators (and, or, not). These concepts are prerequisites because in backtracking, we need to access array elements to compare them and use a boolean array to track usage. Step-by-step: 1. Declare an array: nums = [1,2,3]. 2. Access element: x = nums[1] # x becomes 2. 3. Declare a boolean: flag = False. 4. Change boolean: flag = True. 5. Use in condition: if nums[0] == 1: # do something. Time and space are O(1) for these basic operations. Connection to L4: These operations are used in element comparison and managing the used array. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing (common off-by-one error), accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing boolean values with integers (e.g., using 0/1 instead of False/True). Real code pattern: nums = [1, 2, 3]; element = nums[0]; # gets 1; used = [False, True, False]; if used[1] == True: # condition.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array defined as [5,10,15]. Step 2: Index 2 refers to the third element (since indices start at 0). Step 3: Access nums[2]: value is 15.

examples.input: Boolean variable is_used = False, set to True, then check if True
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize is_used = False. Step 2: Set is_used = True. Now is_used holds True. Step 3: Check condition: if is_used == True: this evaluates to True, so the condition passes.

testCases.input: boolean value=True, check if equal to False
boolean a=True, b=False, a and b

approaches.name: Backtracking with Frequency Map O(n * n!)
approaches.summary: Use a frequency map to count occurrences of each element, then backtrack by decrementing counts to build permutations without duplicates. This method handles duplicates by tracking remaining counts.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique Permutations with Backtracking and Frequency Map
ladders.desc: Complete solution to generate all unique permutations of an array with duplicates using backtracking with frequency map.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the Permutations II problem. The core challenge is generating all possible unique permutations of an array that may contain duplicate elements. The naive approach of generating all permutations and filtering duplicates would be inefficient (O(n! * n) time). Instead, we use backtracking with a frequency map to systematically build permutations while avoiding duplicates. The intuition is to treat each unique element as having a limited number of copies available. We maintain a frequency map counting how many times each element can still be used. During backtracking, we iterate over unique elements (not all positions), and for each element with remaining count > 0, we add it to the current permutation, decrement its count, recurse, then restore the count (backtrack). This ensures we never generate duplicate permutations because identical elements are indistinguishable—we only choose 'which element' not 'which position of identical element'. The algorithm: 1. Build frequency map of array elements. 2. Define recursive backtrack function that takes current permutation and frequency map. 3. Base case: if current permutation length equals original array length, add copy to results. 4. Recursive case: iterate over keys in frequency map (unique elements). For each element with count > 0: append to permutation, decrement count in map, recurse, then remove from permutation and increment count (backtrack). Time complexity: O(n * n!) where n! is number of permutations (worst-case with all unique elements) and n for copying permutations. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and frequency map. Connection to L1: This uses the backtrack helper as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: iterating over original array instead of unique keys (causes duplicates), not making deep copies of results, or incorrect base case condition. Real code pattern: def permuteUnique(nums): freq = Counter(nums); res = []; def backtrack(perm): if len(perm) == len(nums): res.append(perm.copy()); return; for num in list(freq.keys()): if freq[num] > 0: perm.append(num); freq[num] -= 1; backtrack(perm); perm.pop(); freq[num] += 1; backtrack([]); return res.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build frequency map: {1:2, 2:1}. Step 2: Start backtrack with perm=[]. Step 3: First level: choose num=1 (count 2>0). perm=[1], freq={1:1,2:1}. Recurse. Second level: choose num=1 (count 1>0). perm=[1,1], freq={1:0,2:1}. Recurse. Third level: choose num=2 (count 1>0). perm=[1,1,2], freq={1:0,2:0}. Base case: len=3, add [1,1,2] to res. Backtrack: remove 2, freq[2]=1. Backtrack: remove 1, freq[1]=1. Second level: now choose num=2 (count 1>0). perm=[1,2], freq={1:1,2:0}. Recurse. Third level: choose num=1 (count 1>0). perm=[1,2,1], freq={1:0,2:0}. Base case: add [1,2,1]. Backtrack similarly. First level: choose num=2 (count 1>0). perm=[2], freq={1:2,2:0}. Recurse. Second level: choose num=1 (count 2>0). perm=[2,1], freq={1:1,2:0}. Recurse. Third level: choose num=1 (count 1>0). perm=[2,1,1], freq={1:0,2:0}. Base case: add [2,1,1]. Final res: [[1,1,2],[1,2,1],[2,1,1]].

Step 1: freq={1:1,2:1,3:1}. Step 2: backtrack([]). First level: choose 1: perm=[1], freq={1:0,2:1,3:1}. Recurse. Second level: choose 2: perm=[1,2], freq={1:0,2:0,3:1}. Recurse. Third level: choose 3: perm=[1,2,3], freq all 0. Add [1,2,3]. Backtrack. Second level: choose 3: perm=[1,3], freq={1:0,2:1,3:0}. Recurse. Third level: choose 2: perm=[1,3,2]. Add. Similarly explore all branches: after first level choose 2: generates [2,1,3],[2,3,1]; choose 3: generates [3,1,2],[3,2,1]. Total 6 permutations.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtrack Helper Function with Frequency Map
ladders.desc: The recursive backtrack function that builds permutations using a frequency map.
ladders.explanation: This is the heart of the solution: a recursive backtrack function that systematically builds permutations. The function takes two parameters: the current permutation (list) and a frequency map (dictionary). The intuition is to explore all possible choices at each step while ensuring we don't overuse any element. At each recursive call, we check if the current permutation is complete (base case). If not, we iterate over all unique elements in the frequency map. For each element with remaining count > 0, we: 1. Add it to the current permutation. 2. Decrement its count in the frequency map. 3. Recurse to build the rest of the permutation. 4. Remove the element from the permutation (backtrack) and restore its count. This ensures we explore all combinations without duplicates because identical elements are treated as a single choice with limited copies. The key insight is that we iterate over unique elements, not positions—this avoids generating permutations that differ only by swapping identical elements. Time complexity for this subroutine: O(n!) in worst case (all unique elements) as it generates all permutations. Space: O(n) for recursion depth and map. Connection to L0: This function is called by the main solution to generate all permutations. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of using frequency map to handle duplicates. Common mistakes: modifying the frequency map incorrectly (need to decrement before recurse, increment after), not using a copy of map keys to avoid modification during iteration, or incorrect base case. Real code pattern: def backtrack(perm, freq): if len(perm) == target_len: res.append(perm.copy()); return; for num in list(freq.keys()): if freq[num] > 0: perm.append(num); freq[num] -= 1; backtrack(perm, freq); perm.pop(); freq[num] += 1.

examples.trace: Call backtrack([], {1:2,2:1}). First iteration: num=1, freq[1]=2>0. perm=[1], freq={1:1,2:1}. Recurse backtrack([1], {1:1,2:1}). Inside: num=1, freq[1]=1>0. perm=[1,1], freq={1:0,2:1}. Recurse backtrack([1,1], {1:0,2:1}). Inside: num=2, freq[2]=1>0. perm=[1,1,2], freq all 0. Base case: len=3, add [1,1,2]. Return. Backtrack: perm.pop to [1,1], freq[2]=1. Loop ends. Backtrack: perm.pop to [1], freq[1]=1. Next num=2, freq[2]=1>0. perm=[1,2], freq={1:1,2:0}. Recurse backtrack([1,2], {1:1,2:0}). Inside: num=1, freq[1]=1>0. perm=[1,2,1], freq all 0. Add [1,2,1]. Continue similarly for starting with num=2.

backtrack([], {1:1,2:1}). First: num=1, perm=[1], freq={1:0,2:1}. Recurse backtrack([1], {1:0,2:1}). Inside: num=2, perm=[1,2], freq all 0. Add [1,2]. Return. Backtrack: perm=[1], freq[2]=1. Loop ends. Backtrack: perm=[], freq[1]=1. Next num=2, perm=[2], freq={1:1,2:0}. Recurse backtrack([2], {1:1,2:0}). Inside: num=1, perm=[2,1], add [2,1]. Done.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Frequency Map to Handle Duplicates in Backtracking
ladders.desc: The central technique of tracking element counts to avoid duplicate permutations.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight: using a frequency map to handle duplicates during backtracking. The problem with duplicates is that swapping identical elements produces the same permutation. Traditional backtracking that iterates over array indices would generate duplicates. Instead, we maintain a map counting how many times each unique element appears. During backtracking, we choose elements based on their remaining counts, not positions. The intuition: think of each unique element as having a limited supply. When building a permutation, we pick an element from the available supply, use one copy, then recurse. This ensures identical elements are indistinguishable—we don't care which copy we use, just that we use one. The algorithm: 1. Create frequency map from input array. 2. In backtracking, iterate over the keys of the map (unique elements). 3. For each key, if its count > 0, use it (append to permutation, decrement count), recurse, then restore. This guarantees each permutation is generated exactly once because the order of choosing identical elements doesn't matter. Time complexity: building map O(n). Space: O(k) where k is number of unique elements. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the backtrack helper. Connection to L3: It relies on building and updating the frequency map. Common mistakes: using array indices instead of unique keys, not checking count > 0, or incorrectly updating the map. Real code pattern: freq = {}; for num in nums: freq[num] = freq.get(num,0)+1; then in backtrack: for num in freq: if freq[num] > 0: ...

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty map {}. Step 2: Process num=1: map becomes {1:1}. Step 3: Process next num=1: map updates to {1:2}. Step 4: Process num=2: map becomes {1:2, 2:1}. This map tells us we have 2 copies of 1 and 1 copy of 2 available. During backtracking, we'll iterate over keys [1,2]. For key=1: since count=2>0, we can use it. After using, count becomes 1. We can use 1 again later if needed. For key=2: count=1>0, we can use it once. This prevents generating duplicates like [1,2,1] and [1,2,1] from swapping the two 1s.

Step 1: map={}. Step 2: Process 2: map={2:1}. Step 3: Process next 2: map={2:2}. Step 4: Process last 2: map={2:3}. Now during backtracking, we only have one key: 2. We'll check count=3>0, use it, decrement to 2, recurse, etc. Since there's only one unique element, we'll generate only one permutation: [2,2,2]. Without frequency map, naive approach might try to generate multiple identical permutations.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generating All Permutations via Backtracking (Without Duplicates)
ladders.desc: Backtracking algorithm to generate all permutations of an array with unique elements.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental backtracking algorithm for generating permutations when all elements are unique. This is a prerequisite for handling duplicates. The intuition: to generate all permutations, we build them incrementally. At each step, we choose an element that hasn't been used yet, add it to the current permutation, and recurse to build the rest. We need to track which elements have been used to avoid reusing the same element. A common approach is to use a boolean 'used' array or a set. The algorithm: 1. Define recursive function backtrack(perm, used). 2. Base case: if perm length equals array length, add copy to results. 3. Recursive case: iterate over all elements. For each element not used: mark as used, append to perm, recurse, then mark as unused and remove from perm (backtrack). This explores all possible orders. Time complexity: O(n * n!) since there are n! permutations and each takes O(n) to copy. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and used array. Connection to L2: This is extended by replacing 'used' array with frequency map to handle duplicates. Connection to L4: It uses list operations like append and pop. Common mistakes: not making a copy of the permutation when adding to results (leading to reference issues), incorrect base case, or not backtracking properly. Real code pattern: def backtrack(perm, used): if len(perm) == len(nums): res.append(perm.copy()); return; for i in range(len(nums)): if not used[i]: used[i] = True; perm.append(nums[i]); backtrack(perm, used); perm.pop(); used[i] = False.

examples.trace: Start backtrack([], [False,False,False]). First iteration: i=0, nums[0]=1 not used. perm=[1], used=[True,False,False]. Recurse. Inside: i=1, nums[1]=2 not used. perm=[1,2], used=[True,True,False]. Recurse. Inside: i=2, nums[2]=3 not used. perm=[1,2,3], used all True. Base case: add [1,2,3]. Backtrack: used[2]=False, perm=[1,2]. i=2 already tried, loop ends. Backtrack: used[1]=False, perm=[1]. Next i=2, nums[2]=3 not used. perm=[1,3], used=[True,False,True]. Recurse. i=1, nums[1]=2 not used. perm=[1,3,2], add. Continue to explore all branches.

backtrack([], [False,False]). First: i=0, nums[0]=1. perm=[1], used=[True,False]. Recurse. Inside: i=1, nums[1]=2. perm=[1,2], used all True. Add [1,2]. Backtrack: used[1]=False, perm=[1]. Loop ends. Backtrack: used[0]=False, perm=[]. Next i=1, nums[1]=2. perm=[2], used=[False,True]. Recurse. Inside: i=0, nums[0]=1. perm=[2,1], add [2,1]. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Building and Updating a Frequency Map
ladders.desc: Creating and modifying a dictionary to count element occurrences.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operation of building and updating a frequency map (dictionary) that counts how many times each element appears in an array. This is essential for the backtracking approach to handle duplicates. The intuition: a frequency map allows us to track the available copies of each unique element. We start with an empty dictionary. For each element in the array, we check if it's already a key in the dictionary. If yes, we increment its value (count). If no, we add it with count 1. After processing all elements, the dictionary contains each unique element as a key and its frequency as the value. During backtracking, we update this map by decrementing counts when we use an element and incrementing when we backtrack. Time complexity: O(n) to build the map, where n is array length. Space: O(k) where k is number of unique elements. Connection to L3: This map is used instead of a boolean 'used' array in backtracking. Connection to L5: It relies on dictionary operations. Common mistakes: not handling missing keys (use get() or defaultdict), modifying the map incorrectly during iteration, or not resetting counts after backtracking. Real code pattern: freq = {}; for num in nums: freq[num] = freq.get(num, 0) + 1. To update: freq[num] -= 1 (use), freq[num] += 1 (backtrack).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize freq = {}. Step 2: Process num=1: freq.get(1,0)=0, so freq[1]=0+1=1. freq={1:1}. Step 3: Process next num=1: freq.get(1,0)=1, freq[1]=1+1=2. freq={1:2}. Step 4: Process num=2: freq.get(2,0)=0, freq[2]=0+1=1. freq={1:2,2:1}. Step 5: Process num=3: freq.get(3,0)=0, freq[3]=0+1=1. freq={1:2,2:1,3:1}. Step 6: Process last num=1: freq.get(1,0)=2, freq[1]=2+1=3. Final freq={1:3,2:1,3:1}.
Step 1: freq={}. Step 2: Process 5: freq.get(5,0)=0, freq[5]=1. Step 3: Process next 5: freq.get(5,0)=1, freq[5]=2. Step 4: Process last 5: freq.get(5,0)=2, freq[5]=3. Final freq={5:3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Permutations and Recursive Backtracking
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of permutations and recursion with backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational level for beginners. Permutations are all possible arrangements of a set of items. For example, permutations of [1,2] are [1,2] and [2,1]. The number of permutations of n distinct items is n! (n factorial). Recursive backtracking is a technique to explore all possibilities by making choices, recursing, and undoing choices (backtracking) to try other options. The intuition: think of building a solution step by step. At each step, you have several choices. You pick one, move forward, and if you reach a complete solution or a dead end, you go back and try another choice. This is like exploring a tree where each node represents a partial solution. For permutations, we start with an empty list. At step 1, we can choose any element as the first element. After choosing, we recurse to choose the second element from the remaining, and so on. When we've chosen all elements, we have a complete permutation. Then we backtrack to try different choices. Time complexity depends on number of possibilities; for permutations it's O(n!). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L4: This concept is applied with frequency maps. Common mistakes: infinite recursion (missing base case), not backtracking properly, or misunderstanding recursion order. Real code pattern for simple permutation generation: def backtrack(perm, elements): if not elements: res.append(perm); return; for i in range(len(elements)): backtrack(perm + [elements[i]], elements[:i] + elements[i+1:]). This uses list slicing to pass remaining elements.

examples.trace: Start with perm=[], elements=[1,2]. First iteration: i=0, pick elements[0]=1. Call backtrack([1], elements[1:]=[2]). Inside: elements=[2]. i=0, pick 2. Call backtrack([1,2], []). Inside: elements empty, base case: add [1,2] to results. Return. Back to previous level: i loop ends. Return to top level. Next iteration: i=1, pick elements[1]=2. Call backtrack([2], elements[:1]=[1]). Inside: elements=[1]. i=0, pick 1. Call backtrack([2,1], []). Base case: add [2,1]. Done.
Start perm=[], elements=[1]. i=0, pick 1. Call backtrack([1], []). Base case: add [1]. Done.
